topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official notice sat on the worn wooden desk, crisp white paper stark against the faded map of campus tacked above it. Elias traced the embossed lettering with a fingertip, the paper feeling strangely brittle under his touch. Every line of text seemed to vibrate with finality: "Campus operations will cease on May 30th." He reread the sentence three times, the sound of his own breath catching in his throat with each repetition. He pushed back from the desk, the chair scraping loudly against the linoleum floor, a jarring noise in the sudden silence of his small apartment. He walked to the window, staring out at the familiar quad, the vibrant green suddenly looking mocking, an insult to the years he had poured into these ivy-covered walls. His hands clenched into fists at his sides, the tension radiating up his arms, making his knuckles ache. He couldn't bring himself to call anyone, the fear of the inevitable conversation paralyzing him.

[story 2]

"It's just… a restructuring," Sarah mumbled, her voice thin and reedy as she stared at the projected closure timeline flickering on the screen. She kept rubbing her temples, the pressure behind her eyes building into a dull, throbbing ache. She picked at a loose thread on her sweater, her gaze fixed on the pattern, unable to settle on anything else. The air in the room felt thick, heavy, like wading through water. She stood up abruptly, pacing the small confines of the living room, her footsteps quick and uneven. When she finally stopped by the bookshelf, she didn't touch the books; instead, she ran her fingers over the spines, a restless energy churning beneath her skin. A low, involuntary sigh escaped her lips, a sound heavy with unspoken resignation.

[story 3]

I kept staring at my laptop screen, the stark red text mocking me from the display. The world outside the window seemed muted, the colors leached out of the afternoon light. My jaw was tight, muscles knotted beneath my skin. I tried to focus on the complex data models I was supposed to be analyzing, but the numbers swam, dissolving into meaningless shapes. A cold sweat prickled the back of my neck, despite the warmth of the room. I slammed the laptop shut, the sound echoing sharply in the quiet space. I walked to the door, my movements slow and deliberate, each step feeling weighted with an immense, suffocating gravity. I didn't know what to do next, just that staying put felt impossible.
